_meta.system: LANE-018 Slice 3 politics and election bind
_meta.note: Clock remains sole election owner; Verified requires two independent non-tertiary source families.

1862-midterm.title: The 1862 midterms: rebuke, but not a transfer of control
1862-midterm.summary: Opposition Democrats made substantial gains, while the Republican-Unionist coalition retained control of the House.

1864-presidential.supportFormula: clamp(100 - weariness + floor(capital/2) + round(winRate*20), 0, 200); elected at support >= 60
1864-presidential.title: The 1864 referendum: war to victory and abolition, or negotiated peace
1864-presidential.summary: The August nadir and Blind Memorandum gave way after Mobile Bay, Atlanta, and Sheridan's Valley campaign; Lincoln won 212–21 with approximately 55 percent of the popular vote.

US.title: A landslide made from close states
US.text: New York was decided by under one point, Pennsylvania by under four, with Illinois and Indiana also in single digits.

US.title: The soldier vote leaned strongly Lincoln — its total cannot be made exact
US.text: Surviving evidence places Lincoln's military-vote share at 75–80 percent. Counts conflict: 40,247 counted field ballots versus an estimate near 150,000, reflecting partial tallies, home-state counting, and lost records.

US.title: Emancipation reshaped the purpose and politics of the war
US.text: The National Union platform paired unconditional victory with a constitutional amendment abolishing slavery, while racialized opposition attacked emancipation and Black enlistment.

US.title: The election opened an abolition ending; it did not guarantee one
US.text: The Thirteenth Amendment passed the House on January 31, 1865, after failing there in June 1864. It remains an endings and teaching hook, not an election bonus.

CS.title: No Confederate presidential election existed in 1864
CS.text: Jefferson Davis served one six-year term. Wartime legitimacy was contested through Congress, governors, courts, and the home front—not a presidential vote.

CS.title: Dissent inside a slaveholding republic
CS.text: Opposition centered on conscription, impressment, inflation and shortage, habeas corpus, state resistance, and wartime legitimacy. It was not proto-abolitionism; the political order remained committed to slavery.
